name: The Ships That Sing
text:
The ship arrives at the Senn Delta on a morning so clear that fishermen see it from twenty miles out. One vessel, small, moving against the current, making no sound except singing.

The ship is wrong. The wood is alive — grown, not planked. The hull flexes with the water. The sail catches wind that isn't blowing. Seven people stand on deck. They look exhausted in a way that goes past the body into something structural, as if tiredness is built into how they hold themselves.

The singing is coming from the water around the ship — or from the woman at the prow, whose voice moves in patterns that sound less like music and more like conversation. She's talking to the river. The river is answering.

The Brackish make first contact. The woman steps onto the dock, looks at the market, the people, the sky. She puts her hand on the dock-wood and her face changes.

'This wood is dead,' she says, in a language nobody speaks but everyone understands — the words arrive with feeling attached, like a taste on the back of the tongue.

She looks at the sky for a long time. Then at the river. Then at the mountains where the Ashfield sits.

'Your gods,' she says. 'They're so loud. Don't you hear them?'

Nobody answers.

'We can teach you to hear them,' she says. And she means it. And the offer is genuine. And behind her, on the ship, an old woman named Rua is watching the dock with an expression that isn't hope or fear but something between: the look of someone who's arrived at the place they'll either be saved or finally caught.

choices.text: Welcome them. They're clearly exhausted and clearly gifted. Whatever they're running from can wait.
choices.consequence_text:
You spend weeks with the Keepers. They teach you to listen — not to them, to the Weave, the concept god of connection, who has been trying to reach mortals for centuries. The technique is real. It works. The Weave says: finally.

The Keepers are generous with their knowledge and evasive about their past. When you ask where they came from, they say 'across the water.' When you ask why they left, Rua says 'we had to' and changes the subject. When you ask about their gods, the youngest Keeper starts to answer and Rua cuts her off with a look that could stop a river.

They're hiding something. But what they're giving you is genuine. The partnership techniques open a door between mortals and gods that was always there and nobody knew how to use. You can hear the Weave now. It has opinions. It has been lonely.

Whatever the Keepers are running from — it hasn't arrived yet.

events.description: Rua trusts you enough to tell you part of the truth. Not the Unmaking — not yet. But about the homeland. The gods that are gone. The reason they fled. She tells you about the Wind-That-Asks, who chose to die. She doesn't tell you about the ones who didn't choose. She's testing whether you can hold the smaller truth before she gives you the larger one.

choices.text: Help them, but quietly. Don't draw attention. Something about their fear feels real.
choices.consequence_text:
You settle them in the delta, away from the main trade routes. The Brackish help — they're good at discretion, and refugees are a commodity they understand. The Keepers are grateful in a way that makes your chest tight. Not grateful for shelter. Grateful for not being asked questions.

Rua finds you one evening and says, without preamble: 'Something will come after us. Not soon. But it will come. When it does, you'll have to decide what we're worth to you.'

She doesn't explain. She goes back to the ship, where the living wood is slowly dying because the god that grew it is the last god they have, and it's using its strength to keep seven people alive instead of one hull.

events.description: The Keepers' living ship is dying. The wood is going grey. The Listener is spending its essence keeping the crew alive and the hull intact, and it can't do both forever. The ship's death is the first visible sign that the Keepers are carrying something that costs more than they can pay.

choices.text: Ask questions first. Seven people on one ship don't cross an ocean for curiosity. What are they running from?
choices.consequence_text:
Rua looks at you for a long time. She's deciding whether honesty or silence keeps her people safer.

'We saved something,' she says. 'From our own people. Our own people want it back. That's all I can tell you.'

'What did you save?'

She glances at the ocean. The water around the ship is unnaturally still — the kind of still that has a presence behind it, the way a held breath has a person.

'You're standing in front of it,' she says.

You look at the water. The water looks at you. Something vast and patient and older than the river it's sitting in regards you with a curiosity that has weight.

You've just met a god. Not a local god — something from across the ocean, hiding in the Senn Delta, trying to decide if this continent is safe. The answer, it's clear, is: not yet.

events.description: The Listener has decided to trust you. It communicates not in words but in feelings — oceanic feelings, vast and slow. It shows you what happened to the Tideborn pantheon. Not the details. The shape. A dozen points of light going out, one by one, until only one remains. The feeling that accompanies this vision is not grief. It's the thing that comes after grief, when grief has been the only companion for so long it's become architecture.

choices.text: Do nothing. Watch. You've seen enough strangers arrive to know the first reaction is usually wrong.
choices.consequence_text:
You watch from the market. The Brackish negotiate. The Keepers rest. The ship sits in the water and the water around it is strange — too calm, too clear, as if the river is being polite to something.

Over the weeks that follow, the Keepers begin teaching anyone who'll listen. The partnership techniques spread. People who never thought to talk to gods are suddenly hearing them. The Weave speaks to a blacksmith. The Deep murmurs to a fisherman. The continental gods, starved for genuine conversation, pour decades of unheard thoughts into the first mortals who know how to listen.

It's beautiful. It changes everything. And Rua watches it happen with an expression you can't read — something between pride and the look a person gets when they see a fire they lit spreading toward a house.

events.description: The partnership techniques have spread beyond the delta. People across the continent are learning to hear their gods. It's the most significant spiritual development in living memory. The continental gods are revitalized. And somewhere across the ocean, something notices that the Listener has found a continent full of unprotected gods and mortals who are learning to get very, very close to them.

cascade_notes: First wave arrival — the Keepers only. The player builds trust with refugees who are hiding the most dangerous secret in the world. Era 3 brings the majority Tideborn flotilla seeking the Listener and their 'traitors.' Everything the player built with the Keepers is tested when the larger, more impressive, more dangerous group arrives.
